name: King Pleasure
abstract: King Pleasure (born Clarence Beeks; March 24, 1922 – March 21, 1981) was an American jazz vocalist and an early master of vocalese, where a singer sings words to a well-known instrumental solo.

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: If it hadn’t been for King Pleasure... I would not have had the road sign to point me toward lyricizing orchestral versions of things.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 29, 2000 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: When I heard King Pleasure do that, I immediately wrote Four Brothers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: Vocalist Jon Hendricks was born in Newark, Ohio in 1921. He has taken the vocalese style of singing pioneered by King Pleasure and Eddie Jefferson to a virtuoso level
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Giacomo Gates
about_quotes.evidence: His bop-inflected vocal style upholds the tradition pioneered by King Pleasure, Eddie Jefferson and Jon Hendricks.
about_quotes.doc_title: Giacomo Gates interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, March 29, 2015 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Giacomo Gates
about_quotes.evidence: His bop-inflected vocal style upholds the tradition pioneered by King Pleasure, Eddie Jefferson and Jon Hendricks.
about_quotes.doc_title: Giacomo Gates interviewed by Monk Rowe, Utica, New York, November 11, 2017 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: even when King Pleasure came along with the words, “There I go. There I go. There I go” [Moody’s Mood for Love].
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
